claimText: Kharai camels can swim up to 3 km in seawater and survive by grazing on mangroves.
evidenceExcerpt: Kharai camels can swim up to 3 km in seawater, survive by grazing on mangroves
examUse: Statement-based question on unique animal adaptations in India.
distractorHooks: Grazes on alpine pastures instead of mangroves

claimText: The Kharai camel breed is preserved by the Rabari and Fakirani Jat tribes.
evidenceExcerpt: preserved by Rabari and Fakirani Jat tribes.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching tribes to the specific animal breeds they conserve.

claimText: Domestic Bactrian camels are double-humped and found exclusively in the high-altitude cold desert of Ladakh in India.
evidenceExcerpt: Domestic Bactrian camels are found exclusively in the high-altitude cold desert of Ladakh in India.
examUse: Statement-based question on the geographical distribution of camel breeds in India.
distractorHooks: Found in the Thar desert of Rajasthan

claimText: The two humps of Bactrian camels act as energy reservoirs by storing fat, rather than storing water.
evidenceExcerpt: Bactrian camels have two humps acting as energy (fat) reservoirs, not water.
distractorHooks: Humps store water for desert survival
Humps are used for thermoregulation only

claimText: The Wild Bactrian Camel (Camelus ferus) is a distinct species listed as Critically Endangered by the IUCN.
evidenceExcerpt: Wild Bactrian Camel (Camelus ferus) is a distinct species listed as Critically Endangered by IUCN.
examUse: Species-specific question on IUCN conservation status.
distractorHooks: Listed as Extinct in the Wild
Considered the same species as the domestic Bactrian camel
